expanded_description: The coordinate plane is the bridge between algebra and geometry. By assigning coordinates to points, geometric figures can be analyzed with equations, and algebraic relationships can be visualized as shapes. Understanding the axes, quadrants, and how to plot points is the essential first skill for all coordinate geometry—from the Midpoint and Distance Formulas to graphing lines, circles, and parabolas.

core_concept.principle: The coordinate plane is formed by two perpendicular number lines—the x-axis (horizontal) and y-axis (vertical)—intersecting at the origin (0, 0). Every point in the plane is identified by an ordered pair (x, y), where x is the horizontal position and y is the vertical position. The axes divide the plane into four quadrants, each with a distinct sign pattern for coordinates.
core_concept.explanation: Think of the coordinate plane as a map grid laid over the entire plane. The x-axis is like a street running east-west; the y-axis is like a street running north-south. They cross at the origin—the center of the map. Every location has an address: the x-coordinate tells how far east or west you go first, then the y-coordinate tells how far north or south. This 'address system' lets us describe any geometric figure with numbers and equations.

image.alt: A coordinate plane with x-axis (horizontal) and y-axis (vertical) intersecting at the origin (0,0). The four quadrants are labeled I, II, III, IV with their sign patterns: I (+,+), II (−,+), III (−,−), IV (+,−). A point (3,4) is plotted with dashed lines to the axes showing x=3 and y=4.
image.caption: The Coordinate Plane: two perpendicular axes, four quadrants, and points identified by ordered pairs.

sections.introduction: The coordinate plane is built on two perpendicular number lines that intersect at a central reference point. The horizontal axis measures left-right position; the vertical axis measures up-down position. Together they create a grid that assigns every point a unique numerical address.

introduction_image.alt: A coordinate plane with the x-axis highlighted in red (horizontal, labeled with numbers from −5 to 5) and the y-axis highlighted in blue (vertical, labeled from −5 to 5). Both axes have arrowheads indicating infinite extension. The origin (0,0) is circled where they intersect.
introduction_image.caption: The x-axis (horizontal) and y-axis (vertical) are perpendicular number lines intersecting at the origin.

key_concept.definition: The coordinate plane is formed by the x-axis (horizontal number line) and the y-axis (vertical number line), which are perpendicular and intersect at the origin, (0, 0). The x-coordinate of a point measures its horizontal distance from the origin (right is positive, left is negative). The y-coordinate measures its vertical distance (up is positive, down is negative).
key_concept.context: The coordinate plane was invented by René Descartes in the 17th century. It revolutionized mathematics by connecting algebra and geometry. Every point on a line, every vertex of a polygon, and every point on a curve can be described by coordinates and analyzed with equations.

image.alt: A clean coordinate plane. Origin (0,0) labeled. Axes labeled x and y with arrowheads. Positive directions marked: +x (right), −x (left), +y (up), −y (down). A point (4,3) plotted: first move right 4, then up 3. The path is shown with dashed arrows.
image.caption: Coordinates (x, y): move x units horizontally from the origin, then y units vertically.

process_flow.process: Locate the Origin and Orient the Axes
process_flow.description: Identify (0, 0) where the axes cross. Note which axis is x (horizontal) and which is y (vertical). Positive x is to the right; positive y is upward.
process_flow.mechanism: The origin is the reference point for all coordinates. The x-axis runs left-right through the origin. The y-axis runs up-down through the origin. Both extend infinitely in both directions, indicated by arrowheads. Scale the axes with evenly spaced numbers.
process_flow.result: A clear mental map of the coordinate plane with the origin as the reference point.
applications: Always start at the origin when plotting any point.
The sign of x tells direction: + = right, − = left.
The sign of y tells direction: + = up, − = down.

image.alt: Coordinate plane with origin circled and labeled (0,0). Axes labeled x and y with arrowheads. Compass-style directions: +x = East (right), −x = West (left), +y = North (up), −y = South (down).
image.caption: Step 1: Locate the origin. Know your directions—positive x is right, positive y is up.

process_flow.description: From the origin, move right if x is positive or left if x is negative. The distance moved is the absolute value of x.
process_flow.mechanism: The x-coordinate tells the horizontal displacement from the origin. If x = 3, move 3 units right. If x = −4, move 4 units left. If x = 0, stay on the y-axis. Mark your horizontal position mentally—you are now vertically aligned with the point.
process_flow.result: You are now on a vertical line through the point, at the correct x-position.
applications: For (5, 2): move 5 units right from the origin.
For (−3, 4): move 3 units left from the origin.
The x-coordinate always comes first in the ordered pair.

image.alt: A point (5,2) being plotted. From the origin, a red arrow shows moving 5 units to the right along the x-axis. A vertical dashed line extends upward from x=5, showing alignment. Label: 'Step 2: Move x units horizontally.'
image.caption: Step 2: Move horizontally by the x-coordinate. Positive = right, negative = left.

process_flow.description: From your horizontal position, move up if y is positive or down if y is negative. The distance moved is the absolute value of y. Mark the point.
process_flow.mechanism: The y-coordinate tells the vertical displacement from the x-axis. From your current x-position, move y units up (if positive) or down (if negative). If y = 0, stay on the x-axis. Plot a dot at the final location and label it with its coordinates.
process_flow.result: The point is plotted at the intersection of x = constant and y = constant lines.
applications: For (5, 2): from x=5, move 2 units up. Plot (5, 2).
For (−3, −4): from x=−3, move 4 units down. Plot (−3, −4).
Think 'x first, then y' — the alphabet order: x before y.

image.alt: Continuing from Step 2: from the position at x=5, a blue arrow shows moving up 2 units to reach (5,2). The point is plotted as a dot and labeled. A horizontal dashed line extends from the point to the y-axis at y=2. Label: 'Step 3: Move y units vertically. Plot the point.'
image.caption: Step 3: From your x-position, move vertically by the y-coordinate. Plot and label.

classifications.value: A point is written as (x, y). The order matters: x is always first, y is always second.
characteristics: (x, y) — x = horizontal position, y = vertical position
Alphabetical order: x comes before y
(2, 5) and (5, 2) are different points — order is essential
Parentheses and a comma separate the two coordinates
classifications.behavior: The ordered pair is the 'address' of the point. Swapping coordinates moves the point to a different location, often across a diagonal line. Always read and write x first.

examples.explanation:
(3, 7): move right 3, up 7. Located in Quadrant I.

(7, 3): move right 7, up 3. Also Quadrant I, but a completely different point—farther right and lower.

These are NOT the same point. The order of coordinates matters.

image.alt: Two points plotted: (3,7) and (7,3). (3,7) is higher and left. (7,3) is lower and right. They are connected by a dashed arrow labeled 'NOT the same point'. Label: '(3,7) ≠ (7,3) — order matters.'
image.caption: (3, 7) and (7, 3) are different points. The x-coordinate is always first.

classifications.value: Points lying on the x-axis or y-axis have special coordinate patterns and are not in any quadrant.
characteristics: On the x-axis: y = 0 (e.g., (4, 0), (−2, 0)). No vertical displacement from the axis.
On the y-axis: x = 0 (e.g., (0, 5), (0, −3)). No horizontal displacement from the axis.
The origin (0, 0) lies on BOTH axes.
Points on axes are the boundary between quadrants.
classifications.behavior: Points on axes are not assigned to any quadrant. They serve as references—knowing when a coordinate is zero tells you the point lies on an axis.

examples.explanation:
(5, 0): x = 5, y = 0 → lies on the x-axis, to the right of the origin.

(0, −3): x = 0, y = −3 → lies on the y-axis, below the origin.

(0, 0): origin, lies on both axes.

image.alt: Coordinate plane with three points highlighted: (5,0) on the x-axis, (0,−3) on the y-axis, and (0,0) at the origin. Each labeled with its coordinates and the note 'On axis — not in any quadrant.'
image.caption: Points with a zero coordinate lie on an axis, not in any quadrant.

image.alt: Comprehensive diagram: coordinate plane with axes labeled, origin marked, several points plotted in all four quadrants and on axes. Each point shows horizontal and vertical movements from the origin with dashed lines.
image.caption: Plotting points: move x horizontally, then y vertically. The ordered pair (x, y) is the point's address.

sections.introduction: The x-axis and y-axis divide the coordinate plane into four regions called quadrants, numbered I through IV in a counterclockwise direction. Each quadrant has a distinct sign pattern for (x, y) coordinates. Knowing the quadrants helps quickly identify the general location of any point from its coordinates.

introduction_image.alt: Coordinate plane with quadrants labeled I, II, III, IV in large numerals. Quadrant I (top-right) shaded green: (+,+). Quadrant II (top-left) shaded blue: (−,+). Quadrant III (bottom-left) shaded red: (−,−). Quadrant IV (bottom-right) shaded yellow: (+,−).
introduction_image.caption: The four quadrants, numbered counterclockwise. Each has a unique sign pattern for (x, y).

key_concept.definition: The coordinate plane is divided into four quadrants: Quadrant I (x > 0, y > 0), Quadrant II (x < 0, y > 0), Quadrant III (x < 0, y < 0), and Quadrant IV (x > 0, y < 0). The quadrants are numbered in counterclockwise order starting from the upper right. Points on axes are not in any quadrant.
key_concept.context: Knowing quadrant locations is essential for trigonometry (where sine and cosine signs depend on the quadrant), for analyzing functions, and for quickly sketching graphs. The quadrant tells you the signs of both coordinates at a glance.

image.alt: Large coordinate plane with each quadrant numbered and labeled with its sign pattern: I (+,+), II (−,+), III (−,−), IV (+,−). Sample point in each quadrant with coordinates. Axes labeled, origin marked. Counterclockwise numbering direction shown with curved arrow.
image.caption: Quadrant numbering is counterclockwise. Signs: I(+,+), II(−,+), III(−,−), IV(+,−).

classifications.value: The upper-right region where both x and y are positive.
characteristics: x > 0 and y > 0 — both coordinates positive
Located to the right of the y-axis and above the x-axis
Most introductory graphing occurs here
classifications.behavior: Quadrant I is the 'positive-positive' zone. In many applications, data is entirely in Quadrant I (e.g., positive lengths, positive time). All trigonometric functions are positive in Quadrant I.

examples.explanation:
(4, 7): x positive, y positive → Quadrant I.

(0.5, 12): x positive, y positive → Quadrant I.

Any point with both coordinates > 0 belongs here.

image.alt: Quadrant I highlighted. Several points plotted: (4,7), (2,3), (6,1). All have positive x and positive y. The region is shaded light green.
image.caption: Quadrant I: x > 0, y > 0. Both coordinates are positive.

classifications.value: The upper-left region where x is negative and y is positive.
characteristics: x < 0 and y > 0 — x negative, y positive
Located to the left of the y-axis and above the x-axis
classifications.behavior: Quadrant II points go left from the origin (negative x) then up (positive y). In trigonometry, sine is positive here while cosine and tangent are negative.

examples.explanation:
(−4, 7): x negative, y positive → Quadrant II.

(−2, 1): x negative, y positive → Quadrant II.

Negative x, positive y is the signature.

image.alt: Quadrant II highlighted. Points plotted: (−4,7), (−2,3), (−6,1). All have negative x and positive y. The region is shaded light blue.
image.caption: Quadrant II: x < 0, y > 0. Left and up from the origin.

classifications.value: The lower-left region where both x and y are negative.
characteristics: x < 0 and y < 0 — both coordinates negative
Located to the left of the y-axis and below the x-axis
classifications.behavior: Quadrant III is the 'negative-negative' zone. Points are down and left from the origin. In trigonometry, tangent is positive here (ratio of two negatives), while sine and cosine are negative.

examples.explanation:
(−4, −7): x negative, y negative → Quadrant III.

(−1, −3): x negative, y negative → Quadrant III.

Both coordinates negative = Quadrant III.

image.alt: Quadrant III highlighted. Points plotted: (−4,−7), (−2,−3), (−6,−1). All have negative x and negative y. The region is shaded light red.
image.caption: Quadrant III: x < 0, y < 0. Both coordinates are negative.

classifications.value: The lower-right region where x is positive and y is negative.
characteristics: x > 0 and y < 0 — x positive, y negative
Located to the right of the y-axis and below the x-axis
classifications.behavior: Quadrant IV points go right from the origin (positive x) then down (negative y). In trigonometry, cosine is positive here while sine and tangent are negative.

examples.explanation:
(4, −7): x positive, y negative → Quadrant IV.

(6, −1): x positive, y negative → Quadrant IV.

Positive x with negative y is the signature.

image.alt: Quadrant IV highlighted. Points plotted: (4,−7), (2,−3), (6,−1). All have positive x and negative y. The region is shaded light yellow.
image.caption: Quadrant IV: x > 0, y < 0. Right and down from the origin.

image.alt: All four quadrants shown together on one coordinate plane, each in a different color. A summary table: QI (+,+), QII (−,+), QIII (−,−), QIV (+,−). The counterclockwise numbering direction is shown with a curved arrow starting in QI.
image.caption: Complete quadrant overview: numbering, sign patterns, and locations.

sections.introduction: Just as important as plotting points is reading the coordinates of points already on a graph. This skill is used constantly in geometry when analyzing given diagrams, in interpreting data from graphs, and in identifying vertices of polygons in the coordinate plane.

introduction_image.alt: A coordinate plane with several plotted points (not labeled with coordinates). A point in Quadrant I has dashed lines dropping to the x-axis at x=4 and to the y-axis at y=3. Label: 'Read the x-value where the vertical line hits the x-axis. Read the y-value where the horizontal line hits the y-axis.'
introduction_image.caption: Reading coordinates: trace horizontally to the y-axis for y; trace vertically to the x-axis for x.

key_concept.definition: To find the coordinates of a plotted point, draw (or visualize) a vertical line down to the x-axis—the value where it hits is the x-coordinate. Draw a horizontal line across to the y-axis—the value where it hits is the y-coordinate. Write the coordinates as (x, y).
key_concept.context: Reading coordinates is the reverse of plotting. It is used when analyzing pre-drawn figures, checking intersection points, and working with graphs of functions where key points must be identified by their coordinates.

image.alt: A point P plotted in Quadrant I. Dashed vertical line from P to x-axis at 5. Dashed horizontal line from P to y-axis at 4. Arrows show the reading direction. Result: P(5, 4). A second example: Q(−3, 6) in Quadrant II with similar vertical/horizontal tracing.
image.caption: Read x by tracing vertically to the x-axis. Read y by tracing horizontally to the y-axis.

classifications.value: When the point lies on a grid intersection, simply read the x and y values directly from the axes using the grid lines.
characteristics: Easiest when the point is at integer coordinates
Follow the vertical grid line to the x-axis for the x-value
Follow the horizontal grid line to the y-axis for the y-value
Write as (x, y) — x first, then y
classifications.behavior: On standard graph paper, grid lines make reading coordinates simple—the point sits where an x-line and a y-line cross. Identify which vertical line (x) and which horizontal line (y) meet at the point.

examples.explanation:
A point sits on the intersection of the vertical line through x = 3 and the horizontal line through y = −2.

Coordinates: (3, −2).

The point is 3 units right and 2 units down from the origin.

image.alt: A point at the intersection of grid lines x=3 and y=−2. The vertical and horizontal grid lines highlighted. Axes labeled. Point labeled (3, −2).
image.caption: Grid intersection: the point sits where x=3 and y=−2 meet.

classifications.value: When a point does not lie exactly on a grid intersection, estimate its coordinates to the nearest half-unit or tenth, depending on the graph's scale.
characteristics: Used when points fall between grid lines
Estimate the x-coordinate by how far the point is between two vertical grid lines
Estimate the y-coordinate by how far the point is between two horizontal grid lines
The precision depends on the graph's scale and the grid spacing
classifications.behavior: Real-world data points and intersection points often don't fall neatly on grid intersections. Visual estimation to a reasonable decimal is acceptable when exact values aren't given algebraically.

examples.explanation:
A point lies about halfway between the vertical lines x = 2 and x = 3, and slightly above the horizontal line y = 4.

Estimated coordinates: (2.5, 4.2) or approximately (2.5, 4).

Exact coordinates would come from the algebraic equations defining the point.

image.alt: A point plotted between grid lines. Vertical dashed line to x-axis landing between 2 and 3, closer to 2.5. Horizontal dashed line to y-axis landing just above 4. Label: 'Estimate: (2.5, 4.2).'
image.caption: Estimating coordinates when the point is between grid lines.

image.alt: Coordinate plane with several points in different quadrants and on axes. Each has dashed lines to axes showing how to read x and y. A mix of integer and fractional coordinates. Summary: 'Trace vertically for x, horizontally for y.'
image.caption: Reading coordinates: vertical trace → x, horizontal trace → y. Always (x, y).

sections.section_title: Common Mistakes with the Coordinate Plane
sections.introduction: The coordinate plane seems simple, but several errors are common among beginners. Mixing up x and y order, confusing quadrant signs, and misreading negative coordinates can lead to incorrect points and flawed geometric analysis.

key_concept.definition: Common coordinate plane errors include: reversing the order of coordinates (writing (y, x) instead of (x, y)), misidentifying quadrants by confusing sign patterns, starting from the wrong reference point, and misreading axis scales when they are not labeled 1, 2, 3.
key_concept.context: These errors often come from rushing or from not internalizing the conventions. The coordinate plane is a language—once you learn its grammar, these mistakes become easy to spot and correct.

image.alt: Four warning panels: (1) (y,x) vs (x,y) — swapped coordinates point to wrong location. (2) Quadrant sign chart with common confusion: QII is (−,+), not (+,−). (3) Starting from a point other than the origin. (4) Uneven axis scales causing misreading.
image.caption: Common coordinate plane errors: swapped coordinates, wrong quadrant signs, wrong starting point, and misreading scales.

classifications.value: Writing or plotting (y, x) instead of (x, y). This is the most common coordinate error.
characteristics: Error: Plotting (3, 5) by going up 3 and right 5 (treating the first number as y)
Correct: (3, 5) means right 3, up 5 — x first, then y
Result: The point (3, 5) ends up at (5, 3) and vice versa
classifications.behavior: The ordered pair convention is deeply ingrained: x before y, alphabetically. Reversing them reflects the point across the line y = x, which is a completely different location.

examples.explanation:
Correct: (2, 6) means right 2, up 6. Located in Quadrant I, high and left.

Swapped: plotting as right 6, up 2 gives (6, 2). Located in Quadrant I, low and right.

These are completely different points. Always x first, then y.

image.alt: Two points plotted: (2,6) and (6,2). They are clearly in different locations. A diagonal dashed line y=x shown between them. Label: '(2,6) vs. (6,2) — swapping coordinates moves the point across the line y=x.'
image.caption: Swapping x and y reflects the point across the diagonal. (2, 6) ≠ (6, 2).

classifications.note: Solution: Remember 'x before y' alphabetically. The x-axis is horizontal like the crossbar of the letter X. Think: 'x marks the spot horizontally, then y takes you high.'

classifications.value: Misidentifying which quadrant has which sign pattern, especially confusing Quadrant II and Quadrant IV.
characteristics: Error: Thinking (−3, 5) is in Quadrant IV (it's Quadrant II)
Error: Thinking (3, −5) is in Quadrant II (it's Quadrant IV)
Correct: QI (+,+), QII (−,+), QIII (−,−), QIV (+,−)
classifications.behavior: The pattern is systematic: Quadrant I is all positive. Moving counterclockwise, x becomes negative first (QII), then y becomes negative too (QIII), then x becomes positive again while y stays negative (QIV).

examples.explanation:
QII: x is NEGATIVE, y is POSITIVE — left and up: (−,+).

QIV: x is POSITIVE, y is NEGATIVE — right and down: (+,−).

These two are commonly confused. Remember: the sign of x tells left/right; the sign of y tells up/down.

image.alt: Quadrant chart: QI (+,+) with right/up arrows. QII (−,+) with left/up arrows. QIII (−,−) with left/down arrows. QIV (+,−) with right/down arrows. QII and QIV highlighted with warning: 'Commonly confused — check the sign of x.'
image.caption: QII is left and up (−,+). QIV is right and down (+,−). Don't reverse them.

classifications.note: Solution: Think geographically: QI = northeast (both positive), QII = northwest (x negative, y positive), QIII = southwest (both negative), QIV = southeast (x positive, y negative).

classifications.type: Starting from a Point Other Than the Origin
classifications.value: Beginning to count from a previously plotted point instead of returning to the origin.
characteristics: Error: After plotting (2, 3), moving '3 right and 1 up' from (2, 3) to plot (3, 1)
Correct: Each point is independent. (3, 1) means start at (0, 0), move 3 right, 1 up.
This often happens when plotting vertices of a polygon sequentially
classifications.behavior: Each ordered pair is an absolute address measured from the origin, not a relative displacement from the previous point. All points are plotted by starting fresh at (0, 0).

examples.explanation:
To plot triangle vertices A(2, 5), B(6, 3), C(4, 1):

Plot A: start at origin, right 2, up 5.

Plot B: start at origin AGAIN, right 6, up 3. (Do NOT start from A.)

Plot C: start at origin AGAIN, right 4, up 1. (Do NOT start from B.)

image.alt: Two diagrams: (1) Incorrect — points plotted by moving from previous point, resulting in a distorted shape. (2) Correct — each point plotted from the origin, forming the true triangle. Red X on incorrect, green check on correct.
image.caption: Each point is plotted from the origin, not from the previous point.

classifications.note: Solution: For every point, mentally return to (0, 0). 'Reset' to the origin before plotting the next point. Coordinates are absolute positions, not relative movements.

classifications.value: Assuming the scale is always 1 unit per grid line, or misreading axis labels.
characteristics: Error: Counting grid lines instead of reading axis numbers
Error: Assuming each grid line = 1 when the scale might be 2, 5, 10, or 0.5
Correct: Always check the numbers on the axes before counting
classifications.behavior: Axis scales vary depending on the data or the problem. A grid line might represent 0.5, 2, 5, 10, or any value. Never assume—always read the labeled numbers on the axes.

examples.explanation:
If the x-axis is labeled 0, 2, 4, 6, 8... each grid line represents 2 units.

A point (6, 4) is NOT 6 grid lines right and 4 grid lines up—it's 3 grid lines right (6÷2) and 2 grid lines up (4÷2).

Always check: 'What does one grid line represent on this axis?'

image.alt: A coordinate plane where the x-axis is labeled 0, 5, 10, 15 (scale of 5). A point labeled (10, 15) is plotted. A common error: counting 10 grid lines over instead of 2. Diagram shows the correct interpretation.
image.caption: When scale is 5 per grid line, (10, 15) is 2 grid lines right and 3 up—not 10 and 15.

classifications.note: Solution: Before plotting, look at the numbers on the axes. Determine what one grid space represents on each axis. Then divide coordinates by that value to find the number of grid lines to move.

worked_examples.title: Correcting Coordinate Plane Errors
worked_examples.problem:
A student makes these errors. Identify and correct each one.

1) Student plots (4, −2) as left 4, down 2.

2) Student says (−5, 3) is in Quadrant IV.

3) Student plots triangle vertices A(1,2), B(3,5), C(5,1) by moving from A to B and B to C instead of from the origin each time.

4) On a graph where the x-axis is labeled 0, 10, 20..., student plots (30, 15) by counting 30 grid lines right.

step_by_step.explanation:
Error: (4, −2) means x=+4 (right, not left) and y=−2 (down). The student went the wrong direction for x.

Correct: Positive x = right. (4, −2) is 4 units RIGHT and 2 units DOWN — Quadrant IV.

Error: (−5, 3) has x negative (left) and y positive (up). This is Quadrant II, not Quadrant IV.

Correct: QII = (−,+), QIV = (+,−). (−5, 3) is left and up → Quadrant II.

Error: Each point must be plotted from the origin, not from the previous point. Moving from A to B gives B a different position than intended.

Correct: Plot A from origin. Plot B from origin. Plot C from origin. Each is independent. Connect them AFTER all three are plotted.

Error: The scale is 10 units per grid line. (30, 15) is NOT 30 grid lines right—it's 3 grid lines right (30÷10). The y is 1.5 grid lines up.

Correct: Check the axis labels. Divide the coordinate by the scale to find grid lines.

image.alt: Four correction panels: (1) (4,−2) plotted correctly in QIV with right/down arrows. (2) (−5,3) correctly labeled QII. (3) Triangle plotted correctly from origin for each vertex. (4) Graph with scale 10, (30,15) correctly plotted at 3 grid lines right, 1.5 up.
image.caption: Corrected coordinate errors: sign direction, quadrant identification, absolute plotting, and scale reading.

worked_examples.final_answer:
1) (4, −2) = right 4, down 2 (Quadrant IV).

2) (−5, 3) is in Quadrant II (negative x = left, positive y = up).

3) Plot every point from the origin independently.

4) With scale 10, (30, 15) = 3 grid lines right, 1.5 up.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Coordinate conventions: sign = direction, quadrant = sign pattern, origin = absolute reference, scale = axis label interpretation.

sections.introduction: Fluency with the coordinate plane comes from practice plotting points, identifying quadrants, reading coordinates, and connecting plotted points to form geometric figures. Work through these problems to build confidence with the foundational tool of coordinate geometry.

worked_examples.title: Comprehensive Coordinate Plane Practice
worked_examples.problem:
1) Plot and label these points: A(2, 5), B(−3, 4), C(−4, −2), D(6, −1), E(0, 3), F(−5, 0).

2) State the quadrant or axis for each point in problem 1.

3) Three vertices of a rectangle are P(1, 2), Q(5, 2), R(5, 6). Plot them and find the coordinates of the fourth vertex S.

4) A point has x-coordinate −3 and lies in Quadrant III. What is the sign of its y-coordinate? Give an example of such a point.

5) On a graph where each grid line represents 2 units, how many grid lines right and up do you move to plot (8, 6)?

step_by_step.explanation:
A(2, 5): right 2, up 5 — Quadrant I.

B(−3, 4): left 3, up 4 — Quadrant II.

C(−4, −2): left 4, down 2 — Quadrant III.

D(6, −1): right 6, down 1 — Quadrant IV.

E(0, 3): on y-axis (x=0) — not in a quadrant.

F(−5, 0): on x-axis (y=0) — not in a quadrant.

A: QI (both positive). B: QII (x negative, y positive).

C: QIII (both negative). D: QIV (x positive, y negative).

E: y-axis (x = 0). F: x-axis (y = 0).

Plot P(1,2), Q(5,2), R(5,6). P and Q share y=2 (horizontal side). Q and R share x=5 (vertical side).

For a rectangle, the fourth vertex S must share x with P (x=1) and share y with R (y=6).

S = (1, 6). Plot it and verify: PS is vertical, RS is horizontal, forming a rectangle.

Quadrant III: both x and y are negative.

Given x = −3 (already negative), y must also be negative.

Example: (−3, −5), (−3, −1), (−3, −100). Any point with x = −3 and y < 0.

Scale: 2 units per grid line.

For (8, 6): x = 8, number of grid lines = 8 ÷ 2 = 4 right.

y = 6, number of grid lines = 6 ÷ 2 = 3 up.

Plot by moving 4 grid lines right and 3 grid lines up.

image.alt: Multiple diagrams: (1) Six points plotted and labeled A through F. (2) Same points with quadrant labels. (3) Rectangle P, Q, R with fourth vertex S(1,6) found. (4) Quadrant III with example (−3,−5). (5) Graph with scale 2, showing (8,6) at 4 grid lines right and 3 up.
image.caption: Comprehensive coordinate plane practice: plotting, quadrants, geometric reasoning, and scale interpretation.

worked_examples.final_answer:
1) Points plotted: A(2,5), B(−3,4), C(−4,−2), D(6,−1), E(0,3), F(−5,0).

2) A: QI, B: QII, C: QIII, D: QIV, E: y-axis, F: x-axis.

3) S(1, 6).

4) y is negative. Example: (−3, −5).

5) 4 grid lines right, 3 grid lines up.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Plotting from origin, quadrant sign patterns, geometric reasoning on the plane, and interpreting axis scales.

summary: The coordinate plane is the foundation of coordinate geometry, bridging algebra and geometry. Key points: 1) Axes: The x-axis (horizontal) and y-axis (vertical) are perpendicular number lines intersecting at the origin (0, 0). Positive x is right; positive y is up. 2) Plotting Points: An ordered pair (x, y) gives the point's location. Start at the origin. Move horizontally by x (right if positive, left if negative). Then move vertically by y (up if positive, down if negative). The x-coordinate always comes first—alphabetical order: x before y. 3) Four Quadrants: The axes divide the plane into Quadrant I (+,+), Quadrant II (−,+), Quadrant III (−,−), and Quadrant IV (+,−), numbered counterclockwise from the upper right. Points on axes (x=0 or y=0) are not in any quadrant. 4) Reading Coordinates: From a plotted point, trace vertically to the x-axis for the x-coordinate and horizontally to the y-axis for the y-coordinate. Write as (x, y). 5) Common Errors: Swapping x and y order (the most frequent error), confusing quadrant signs (QII is −,+; QIV is +,−), plotting points relative to each other instead of from the origin, and misreading axis scales. 6) This coordinate system is the language for all coordinate geometry topics—Midpoint Formula, Distance Formula, Slope, equations of lines and circles, and graphing functions.

review_questions_image.alt: Concept map with 'Coordinate Plane' in center. Branches: Axes (x-axis horizontal, y-axis vertical, origin (0,0)), Quadrants (I (+,+), II (−,+), III (−,−), IV (+,−), counterclockwise numbering), Plotting Points (start at origin, move x horizontally, move y vertically, ordered pair (x,y)), Reading Coordinates (trace to axes), Common Errors (swapping x/y, quadrant signs, relative plotting, scale misreading).
